← the Atlas

Remote terminals over SSH

Features· budding ·accepted ·JUN 21, 2026

The skeleton plan for kolu#951 — remote terminals over SSH, as one R1–R5 phase tree. Foundation (R1) and the local kaval survivor (R2) shipped; remote-over-ssh (R3), the arivu awareness fold (R4), and the overflow-recovery loop (R5) remain. Depth lives in the child notes — pty-daemon (R2), kaval-sessions (R3), arivu (R4).

Contents

  • User-facing description
  • Architecture-level changes
  • Implementation details
  • History

The skeleton plan for #951 — remote terminals over SSH. Every host — this machine and each ssh remote — runs the same kaval daemon; kolu-server keys its terminal endpoint by hostId, and you switch hosts from the ChromeBar, kinda like tmux sessions. R1 (foundation) and R2 (the local kaval survivor) are shipped; R3 (remote over ssh), R4 (the arivu awareness fold), and R5 (the overflow-recovery loop) remain. This note is the spine — the phase tree and the cross-host shape. The depth lives in the child notes: pty-daemon is R2, kaval-sessions is R3, arivu is R4.

User-facing description

kolu
hostslocalnix@prodstaging+ host⟳ live
local
kolu · master
◜codexworking
nix@prodssh
kolu · feat/dial-ssh #1412 ✓
●claudeawaiting you
nix@prodssh
infra · deploy
◜—working
local
notes · main
○claudeidle

You switch hosts from the ChromeBar — kinda like tmux sessions — and a remote host’s terminals sit on the canvas beside your local ones. A remote tile wears an ssh badge and carries the same live awareness a local tile does — git branch/dirty, PR status, agent state, and a green dot when it’s moving bytes — and it survives a network blip or a kolu-server deploy with full scrollback. One local host is always present; remotes are added on demand, auto-detected from your ssh config.

Architecture-level changes

One endpoint, bound to a dialed kaval. A kaval is a daemon you dial — local over a unix socket, remote over ssh — so remote is not a second backend. There is one TerminalEndpoint, bound to a HostLocation-resolved kaval endpoint ({kind:"local"} today, {kind:"remote",host} in R3). That resolver is the sole place a tile maps to its kaval; everything downstream talks to the backend and never asks “which kind?”. No RemoteTerminalEndpoint — R3 adds the ssh driver as an additive sibling of R2’s local driver, never a retrofit.

Two volatility axes stay open (R1/R2 closed the rest):

Axis — changes for its own reasonEncapsulated by
Where a terminal’s state lives — this machine vs an ssh hostthe HostLocation-resolved kaval endpoint — one impl + a discriminator, not a second backend
How the backend reaches its agent — transport · framing · reconnectHostSession + @kolu/surface/links/stdio — unix socket today, ssh in R3

Zero domain knowledge crosses the transport (Hickey’s cut): the agent runs the unmodified sensors and the boundary ships their values. An earlier per-domain design (RemoteGitInfoProvider, …) was transport adapters wearing domain costumes; the single-endpoint seam dissolved them.

The awareness fold is domain logic, not electricity

R4’s fold merges each remote AwarenessValue into the co-owned terminalMetadata entry and splits it across the persisted/live fence — pr·agent·foreground through the quiet live mutator, cwd·git·lastAgentCommand·lastActivityAt through the one that arms autosave. Lose the split and you either re-arm the agent-firehose (a perf regression) or stop persisting. It speaks the whole terminal domain and the autosave fence, so it stays inline in kolu-server — never a @kolu/* package.

Already paid for, so R3–R5 are narrow. @kolu/surface-nix-host ships HostSession, provisionAgent (ships the derivation — a darwin parent drives a linux remote with no cross-builder), and resolveSystem; @kolu/surface ships mirrorRemoteSurface (the per-primitive mirror). R2 already shipped the host-count-agnostic spine: @kolu/surface-daemon-supervisor and the per-connect system.version handshake. R3 is a driver behind a shipped seam; R4 a fold behind a shipped mirror; R5 one wire signal.

Implementation details

Every phase done so far, as one tree — R1 and R2 shipped, R3–R5 remain. Each big node links to its child note for the PR-level build log.

REMOTE TERMINALS OVER SSH · #951
✓R1 · Foundationshipped
✓R1a · TerminalEndpoint seam#981
✓R1b · @kolu/surface + surface-nix-host#984
✓R1c · @kolu/terminal-awareness engine#1004
✓R2 · kaval — local PTY survival→ pty-daemon · shipped
✓R2.1 · spawn-policy inversion#1292
✓R2.2 · kaval binary + client#1301
✓R2.3 · the door (topology flip)#1310
✓R2.4 · survival chain#1330·37·44·53
✓R2.5 · live inventory#1458
◐R3 · Remote over ssh→ kaval-sessions · underway
✓R3.1 · backend cleanup#1364
✓R3.2 · kaval-tui create#1370
✓R3.3 · kaval-tui --host (reach + provision)#1373
✓R3.4 · upstream frontDaemonOverStdio#1374
✓R3.5 · skip redundant provisioning#1377
✓R3.6 · multiplex ssh connections#1378
○R3.7 · kolu dials remotes (the ssh driver)next
○R3.8 · canvas multiplex + validatenext
◐R4 · Remote awareness (the arivu fold)→ arivu · underway
✓R4.1 · extract @kolu/terminal-awareness#1413
✓R4.2 · provider → adapter rename#1419
✓R4.3 · arivu + arivu-tui standalone#1428
✓R4.4 · arivu-tui --host#1439
✓R4.5 · arivu-tui fleet dashboard#1470·79·86·97
○R4.6 · the kolu mirror + foldgated on R3
○R5 · Overflow-recovery loop~130 LoC · last

R3 — remote over ssh (kaval-sessions). The spike shipped (R3.1–R3.6): a kaval is now dialable local-or-ssh from kaval-tui, with the warm ssh path provisioned, deduped, and connection-multiplexed ( #1373 · #1377 · #1378 ). What’s left: R3.7 — kolu-server’s N-endpoint registry + the ssh driver (the {kind:"remote",host} arm, the (host,id) join, a persisted location), reaching the remote pair (a kolu-watcher fronting the host’s kaval); R3.8 — the canvas multiplex (a tile per kaval, local always present) and validating the feel against tmux-switch.

R4 — remote awareness fold (arivu). The awareness daemon shipped standalone (R4.1–R4.5): the sensor library, arivu + arivu-tui, --host, and the live multi-host fleet board ( #1428 · #1439 · #1497 ). R4.6 is the kolu consumer: dial a remote arivu over a long-lived HostSession, mirror its awareness collection with mirrorRemoteSurface, and fold each AwarenessValue into terminalMetadata across the persisted/live fence (see the callout above), stamping location kolu-side. Four irreducible, remote-only costs: closure→frame reification (already shipped, R4.3), the fold + fence split, semantic version-skew (bounded by re-provision-on-dial), and the kolu-side location stamp. Gated on R3 — there’s no remote tile to fold into until R3 ships one.

R5 — overflow-recovery loop (~130 LoC w/ tests). kaval sheds a slow attach subscriber by ending its iterator — today indistinguishable on the wire from a PTY exit, so the client treats the drop as terminal and silently freezes scrollback (onOverflow exists but is unwired). Thread a typed overflow → re-attach signal through the attach contract so the client re-attaches for a fresh snapshot (reusing the existing onRetry scroll-lock reset), distinct from PTY-exit. Open: whether a drop is reachable on the local socket at all, or only across an ssh hop — if the latter, R5 collapses into an R3 companion.

History

  • 2026-05-26 → 28 — R1 shipped: the TerminalEndpoint seam ( #981 ), the framework gaps + @kolu/surface-nix-host ( #984 ), and the shared sensor engine ( #1004 ).
  • R2 re-sequenced before R3/R5 — a whole-plan /lowy pass showed that building the multi-client survivor once collapses the later phases; R2 split into pty-daemon and shipped end-to-end (R2.1 → R2.5), closing #671 (terminals survive a deploy).
  • Multi-host end state set 2026-06-11: 1 local + N ssh kavals, host-switched in the ChromeBar; R2’s redo baked the host-count-agnostic shapes (hostId-keyed status, the HostLocation discriminator) so R3 retrofits nothing.
  • Renumbered to one R1–R5 tree (2026-06-21) — was R-1/R-1.5/R-4/R-2/R-3 plus colliding child P/B schemes; git holds the old labels. Cf. ghostex vs kolu, herdr vs kolu, kaval vs zmosh.

Referenced by

  • arivu — terminal awareness as an ephemeral surface on kaval
  • Ghostex vs. kolu remote-terminals
  • Kaval sessions — dial daemons, multiplex the canvas
  • R2 — kaval, the standalone PTY daemon
